summary: This abstract outlines Terrie E. Moffitt's developmental taxonomy of antisocial behavior, distinguishing between life-course-persistent and adolescence-limited offenders. It details the distinct etiologies, trajectories, and characteristics of each group, noting that life-course-persistent behavior is rooted in neurodevelopmental issues while adolescence-limited behavior is a near-normative response to the maturity gap.

principles.statement: Antisocial behavior follows two primary developmental prototypes: life-course-persistent and adolescence-limited.
principles.rationale: Research supports a taxonomic theory where these two groups differ fundamentally in their origins, duration, and underlying pathology.
principles.source_quote: This chapter reviews 10 years of research into a developmental taxonomy of antisocial behavior that proposed two primary hypothetical prototypes: life-course-persistent versus adolescence-limited offenders.

principles.statement: Life-course-persistent antisocial behavior is pathological, rare, and rooted in early neurodevelopmental and family adversity.
principles.rationale: This type of behavior begins in childhood, has strong genetic etiological contributions, and continues persistently into adulthood, often associated with serious offending and violence.
principles.source_quote: According to the taxonomic theory, life-course-persistent offenders' antisocial behavior has its origins in neurodevelopmental processes; it begins in childhood and continues persistently thereafter.

principles.statement: Adolescence-limited antisocial behavior is common, transient, and near normative.
principles.rationale: It originates from social processes rather than neurodevelopmental pathology, begins in adolescence, and typically desists in young adulthood.
principles.source_quote: Adolescence-limited antisocial individuals are common, relatively transient, and near normative

principles.statement: Abstaining from delinquency during adolescence is rare and often indicates social exclusion.
principles.rationale: Because adolescence-limited antisocial behavior is so common and near normative, those who completely abstain are typically excluded from normative peer group activities.
principles.source_quote: the hypothesis that abstainers from delinquency are rare individuals who are excluded from normative peer group activities in adolescence

frameworks.name: Developmental Taxonomy of Antisocial Behavior
frameworks.when_to_apply: When classifying, diagnosing, or predicting the trajectory of antisocial and criminal behavior in individuals over their lifespan.
steps: Identify the onset of the antisocial behavior (childhood vs. adolescence).
Assess the underlying etiology (neurodevelopmental/genetic risk factors vs. social processes/maturity gap).
Predict the developmental trajectory (persistent into adulthood with serious offending vs. transient and desisting in young adulthood).
frameworks.source_quote: This chapter reviews 10 years of research into a developmental taxonomy of antisocial behavior that proposed two primary hypothetical prototypes: life-course-persistent versus adolescence-limited offenders.

mental_models.name: The Maturity Gap and Social Mimicry
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding why otherwise healthy adolescents engage in antisocial behavior. They experience a 'maturity gap' and engage in 'social mimicry' of antisocial models to bridge the gap between biological maturity and social independence.

mental_models.name: Life-Course-Persistent vs. Adolescence-Limited Prototypes
mental_models.description: A dual-pathway model for analyzing antisocial populations, separating a small, pathological group whose behavior is driven by neurodevelopmental deficits from a large, normative group whose behavior is driven by transient social factors.

signature_quotes.text: According to the taxonomic theory, life-course-persistent offenders' antisocial behavior has its origins in neurodevelopmental processes; it begins in childhood and continues persistently thereafter.
signature_quotes.context: Defining the origins and trajectory of the life-course-persistent prototype.

signature_quotes.text: Adolescence-limited antisocial individuals are common, relatively transient, and near normative
signature_quotes.context: Describing the nature of the adolescence-limited prototype.

signature_quotes.text: abstainers from delinquency are rare individuals who are excluded from normative peer group activities in adolescence
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the social position of adolescents who do not engage in any antisocial behavior.
